Know their religion. What does that mean? This is a deeper dive into tactical empathy into what builds insane amounts of influence. It extends beyond trust- based influence and into practically unconscious influence. It's about what drives people so deeply they've almost become blind to it. Everyone, and I mean everyone, has things they believe in that are larger than what's at stake in a given moment. Their religion, this is what's bigger than they are, what they believe in. It gives them a greater identity and transcends them. These concepts are largely evidenced by who they see themselves as, as human beings, and their worldview. As we've discussed, if you can trigger the sense in someone that you see what they see, that you understand them as people, then you create a bond and immediately gain influence. They feel less alone. They resonate in a unique way with you. Even with nothing in common, showing your counterpart that you understand their worldview will have an unmistakably positive effect on them. Understanding the other side's worldview, their reason for being, is the key to understanding their religion. Here are a few examples of what we mean when we use the term religion. In hostage negotiation, a barricaded man makes continued references to being in the 82nd Airborne. He says the 82nd can retreat after 72 hours if they receive no reinforcements. He uses this as a reason for waiting for three days before he surrenders. Scattered throughout his dialogue are Christian references. The negotiator points out to him that slightly after 48 hours into the barricade, it becomes the dawn of the third day, a Christian term associated with Jesus. The barricaded man agrees to come out after 48 hours instead of 72. The negotiator achieved his goal of an earlier surrender by tapping into the barricaded subject's religious aspirations. In business negotiation, a businessman makes continued references to his hunting passions in conversations with the potential consultant being considered for a special project. The businessman's other advisors are telling him he doesn't need this consultant for this particular project. Contained within the adjectives about hunting are occasional born-again Christian references. When the consultant says to the businessman, this sounds like a stewardship for you. A term often used by meaning the Christian faith, he responds with, you understand me, you're hired. In the business of sports, Ted Leonsis, the owner of the Washington Capitals hockey team, consistently talks about creating the immortal moments in sports that fans will tell their grandchildren about. When professional athletes hear him say this, the message they hear is that Mr. Leonsis wants to make them immortal. While he pays them fairly well, his players don't hold out on their contracts and his teams consistently win and make the playoffs because he taps into their desire for immortality. So your counterparts religion is something that dying for or figuratively giving their last breath for will essentially put them on a path to immortality or even better, living for that purpose grants them immortality and living martyr status or living hero status. Even if their religion is simply themselves, they will lust for living hero status. After all, what's better than both gaining this status and being alive to enjoy it, right? If actions can be redefined and perceived to be putting them on that path, giving them that status, then massive and sometimes instantaneous changes in decision making are possible. If you can position your objectives within their worldview, within the religion that they use to make decisions, then you've gained an extremely valuable tool. What are some tips for reading religion correctly? Review the adjectives that they use. The words they select to give meaning to other words and ideas will provide great clues. There will often be a surface group of adjectives or more common lingo they draw from, but then scattered throughout will be descriptors that indicate another theme or philosophy. These are the words they use to convey passion and often will be the most sincere and consequently revealing. Which skills should you use when you hear these words? Marrying these words will be enormously helpful. Since you may not hear everything the first time, double check with your team members. You will often discover new information that will help you advance the negotiation. Use backup listeners whose only job is to listen between the lines. Backup listeners catch the things you miss. Understanding their religion is a great way to provide you with powerful influence which leads to great negotiating outcomes.
